title: Map fold page design for a journal
subtitle: Folding a large sheet down to journal size for an expandable spread
excerpt: A map fold lets you include a full A3 or A4 page inside an A5 journal by folding it to the journal's dimensions. The folded page sits flush with the other pages but opens out to a larger working surface: useful for sketched plans, timelines, or reference material that needs more space than a single journal page.
sourceNotes: Technique synthesised from general origami folding practice and from the map and broadside folding conventions used in bookbinding and printed ephemera.

glossaryTerms.definition: A folding method in which a large rectangular sheet is folded in alternating directions to reduce it to a smaller rectangular form that unfolds flat in one movement. Named after the way printed maps are traditionally folded for pocket storage.

content.text: inserts a page much larger than the journal into the binding without it protruding. The sheet folds down to match the journal's dimensions and, when taped or pasted to a regular journal page at one edge, behaves as a pocket insert: flat when the journal is closed, openable to its full extent when needed. The folding itself takes less than five minutes once the fold lines are scored.

items.name: Sheet to fold, 80-120 gsm
items.description: Any paper that folds cleanly. Thin paper (80 gsm) shows the fold lines less; heavier paper holds its shape better when open. Cut to an exact multiple of the journal page size before folding.

items.name: Bone folder (slug: bone-folder)
items.description: Score each fold line before folding. A scored fold is sharper and does not crack the paper surface.

content.text: For an A5 journal (148 mm wide), an A4 sheet (210 mm wide) needs one fold along its width to reduce it to approximately 105 mm: slightly narrower than the journal, which is correct. An A3 sheet (297 mm wide) folds to A5 width in two accordion folds at 148 mm intervals. The height of the insert should already match the journal height, or trim it to match before folding.

Lay the sheet face-down on a flat surface. Mark the fold positions lightly in pencil on the back. Place the metal ruler along each mark and run the bone folder along it with firm, even pressure. Score all the fold lines before you begin folding: this ensures each fold goes exactly where planned rather than wandering as successive folds shift the sheet.

Fold the sheet so that alternating panels fold toward you (valley fold) and away from you (mountain fold). For a two-panel map fold: fold the right third back behind the sheet; fold the left third forward over the top. The result is three panels stacked into the width of one, opening from right to left. For a four-panel fold on a wider sheet, alternate valley-mountain-valley across the width. Press each fold firmly before moving to the next.
Attaching the insert to the journal

Attach the folded insert to the journal by applying a strip of PVA glue along the spine edge of the insert and pressing it flat against the gutter of a journal page. Leave to dry under weight. The insert opens fully outward without disturbing the binding. Alternatively, tip the insert in as a loose leaf behind a page and secure with a paper clip; this allows removal without adhesive.
